title: Protocol for intra-nerve AAV injection and dorsal root potential recording for optogenetic modulation of the peripheral sensory nerve activity
author: Kosugi, Sidikejiang, et al.
description: SummaryOptogenetic modulation of peripheral sensory nerve activity holds great potential for revealing the mechanisms underlying sensory disorders, with important implications for developing therapeutic strategies. Here, we present a protocol for applying optogenetic techniques to peripheral sensory nerves using an adeno-associated virus (AAV) vector. We describe the procedure for gene transduction into dorsal root ganglion neurons via retrograde transport following intra-nerve AAV injection. We then outline a terminal, acute electrophysiological experiment to evaluate optogenetic effects at the dorsal root level.For complete details on the use and execution of this protocol, please refer to Kosugi et al.1

steps_raw.title: Before you begin
steps_raw.instruction: The protocol below describes the specific steps for imparting optogenetic capabilities to modulate peripheral sensory nerve activity. Optogenetics is the combination of genetic and optical methods to control neuronal activity using light-sensitive proteins, known as opsins.2,3 Depending on the type of opsin, light irradiation can depolarize or hyperpolarize the neuronal membrane potential, thereby enabling excitation or inhibition of neural activity. Due to the heterogeneity of peripheral sensory nerves, it is crucial to parse out the contributions of functional types of sensory afferents, which necessitates the use of electrophysiological methods. Compound action potentials recorded from the dorsal root of the spinal cord (i.e., dorsal root potentials) provide a reliable electrophysiological readout for assessing both the number and functional type of modulated afferent fibers.4,5,6,7 Here, we provide a comprehensive procedure for imparting optogenetic capabilites to peripheral sensory nerves using an adeno-associated virus (AAV) vector, along with methods for precisely evaluating its effects. Specifically, we describe the injection of AAV into the sciatic nerve and the subsequent recording of dorsal root potentials at the lumbar level in male Wistar rats. This protocol can be readily adapted for other nerves and animal models, such as the common marmoset. For AAV injection procedures in marmosets, see Kudo et al.8

steps_raw.title: Preparation for viral injection
steps_raw.instruction:
Timing: >1 week before (for steps 1–3)
Timing: >1 day before (for step 4)

steps_raw.title: Preparation for in vivo dorsal root recording
steps_raw.instruction:
Timing: 5 h
Timing: 4 weeks after steps 1–28

steps_raw.title: Preparation for in vivo dorsal root recording
steps_raw.instruction: This position of the protocol details the procedure for preparation of dorsal root potential recording at the lumber level.Note: Based on our evaluation,1 no clear differences were observed between 3 and 8 weeks after AAV injection. Therefore, 4 weeks post-injection was adopted as the standard time point.29.Inject the rat intraperitoneally with 0.2 mg/kg medetomidine, 2.0 mg/kg midazolam, and 2.5 mg/kg butorphanol.30.Verify adequate depth of anesthesia by monitoring pupil size and flexion reflex to the paw pinch.31.Shave the back (from the caudal end of the sternum to the tail), the ventral surface (from the larynx to the abdomen), and both thighs (ventral and dorsal sides).32.Put the animal on the heating pad and fixed in a supine position.Note: Position the head as flat as possible to keep the trachea horizontal. If necessary, place a paper towel or similar support under the neck to maintain alignment.33.Bind both hands and feet.34.Begin monitoring heart rate, oxygen saturation, and rectal temperature. During surgery, heart rate is maintained within the range of 200–350 beats per minute, oxygen saturation is maintained above 95%, and core temperature is maintained between 35–38°C.35.Start tracheotomy. First, make a 20 mm incision in the skin above the trachea, at the level between the ear and the shoulders (Figure 4A).Figure 4Detailed method for tracheotomy(A) Location of the incision.(B) Exposure of the trachea.(C) Isolation of the trachea, as indicated by the square in (B).(D) Making an incision in the trachea.(E) Insertion of the Y-shaped tube into the trachea.(F) Securing the tube by tying the suture.(G) Further securing the tube by tying the suture.(H) Placement of a loop suture for stabilization.(I) Skin closure with non-absorbable suture.Note: Tracheotomy is necessary to administer rocuronium bromide for neuromuscular blockade, which is necessary to suppress reflexive muscle activity during dorsal root recording. Because neuromuscular blockade compromises spontaneous respiration, tracheotomy is performed in advance to secure the airway.36.Expose and isolate the trachea from the surrounding tissue (Figure 4B).37.Lift the trachea and pass two 2-0 braided silk sutures underneath it (Figure 4C).38.Make an incision in the trachea between the two sutures (Figure 4 D).Note: Take care to prevent blood or other fluids from entering the trachea through the incision site. It is recommended to gently lift the trachea by holding up 2-0 braided silk sutures placed underneath it.39.Insert a Y-shaped tube into the tracheal incision (Figure 4 E).Note: To prevent the tip from contacting the tracheal wall and causing an obstruction, ensure that the Y-tube is inserted horizontally.40.Tie the sutures around the trachea to secure the Y-shaped tube (Figure 4F).Note: Tie the caudal suture first, then tie the rostral suture. Finally, tie the two sutures together.41.Place and tie a 2-0 braided silk suture to the skin at the bifurcation of the Y-shaped tube, then wrap it around the tube to further secure it (Figure 4G).42.Loop the suture around the bifurcation again and tie it to the previously placed sutures (Figure 4H).43.Close the skin with a non-absorbable suture (Figure 4I).44.Connect the Y-shaped tube to the ventilator.45.Start mechanical ventilation.Note: Carefully monitor for synchronization between chest movements and ventilator activity.46.Switch the anesthesia to 0.5–1% isoflurane.47.Begin establishing an intravenous line. First, make a 20 mm incision on the medial thigh skin (Figure 5A).Figure 5Detailed method for establishing an intravenous line(A) Location of the incision.(B) Placement of a retractor.(C) Exposure of the femoral vein, as indicated by the square in (B).(D) Isolation of the vein.(E) Ligation of the vein branch.(F) Ligation of the vein using micro serrefines.(G) Insertion of the catheter.(H) Securing the catheter by tying the suture.(I) Further securing the catheter by tying the suture.(J) Skin closure with non-absorbable suture.Note: Intravenous line placement is required to administer rocuronium bromide intravenously for neuromuscular blockade, which is necessary to suppress reflexive muscle activity during dorsal root recording.48.Open the incision site with a retractor to ensure adequate exposure (Figure 5B).49.Expose and isolate the femoral vein from the surrounding tissue (Figure 5C).Note: When exposing the femoral vein, dissect as proximally as possible. The vein is thicker closer to the proximal end, making catheter insertion easier. Take care not to damage the artery, as it runs immediately adjacent to the vein.50.Ligate the femoral vein branches (Figure 5D).Note: The more branches are ligated, and a straight segment is ensured, the easier it becomes to insert the catheter.51.Lift the femoral vein and pass two 4-0 braided silk sutures underneath it (Figure 5E).Note: Take care to avoid twisting the vein when passing the 4-0 braided silk suture under the vein.52.Clamp the femoral vein proximal to the sutures using a micro serrefine (Figure 5F).Note: Clamp the proximal end first to engorge the vessel.53.Ligate the distal portion of the femoral vein with a suture (Figure 5F).54.Make an incision just proximal to the ligature and insert a catheter (Figure 5G).Note: Grasp the vessel incision with fine forceps and use curved fine forceps to handle the catheter. If the catheter cannot be inserted smoothly, withdraw it slightly. Failure to do so may result in vessel rupture. If the clamp is loose or a branch is overlooked, blood may continue to flow. In such cases, ligate the suture on the proximal side immediately to stop the blood flow.55.Tie the sutures around the vein to secure the catheter (Figure 5H).Note: Tie the proximal suture first, then tie the distal suture. Finally, tie the two sutures together.56.Place and tie a 2-0 braided silk suture to the skin at the distal end of the incision, then wrap it around the catheter to further secure it (Figure 5I).57.Slightly withdraw the syringe to check for blood backflow.58.Close the skin with a non-absorbable suture (Figure 5J).59.Secure the catheter to the thigh with surgical tape to prevent dislodgement (Figure 5J).60.Turn the animal over and place it in the prone position.Note: It is recommended to temporarily remove the electrocardiogram, oxygen saturation, and rectal temperature probes as well as the ventilator tubing.61.Begin the laminectomy. First make an incision in the skin from just above the floating rib to just above the iliac crest (Figure 6A).Figure 6Detailed method for laminectomy(A) Location of the incision.(B) Exposure of the spinous processes.(C) Exposure of the articular processes, as indicated by the square in (B).(D) Exposure of the spinal cord by laminectomy.(E) Exposure of the dorsal root ganglia (DRG).(F) Isolation of the dorsal root.(G) Mounting a bipolar hook electrode.(H) Placement of a multimode optical fiber above the DRG.(I) Location of the incision.(J) Placement of a retractor.(K) Isolation of the sciatic nerve, as indicated by the square in (J).(L) Mounting a bipolar cuff electrode.(M) Placement of the optical fiber above the nerve.62.Expose the spinous processes from the 13th thoracic vertebra (Th13) to the 1st sacral vertebra (S1) by dissecting the erector spinae muscles (Figure 6B).Note: Segmental levels are determined based on vertebral mobility. Vertebrae below S1 lack intervertebral motion and are thus considered immobile.63.Expose the articular processes using fine scissors, bone curette, and micro curette (Figure 6C).64.Perform a laminectomy from the 1st lumbar vertebra (L1) to S1 (Figure 6D).Note: Holding the vertebral body with a toothed forceps and creating sufficient space while maintaining spinal extension allows safe insertion of a bone rongeur without risking damage to the spinal cord.65.Expose the 4th (L4) and 5th (L5) lumbar DRGs (Figure 6E).CRITICAL: The region around the DRG is particularly prone to bleeding, which can obstruct light delivery and prevent the recording of responses to DRG irradiation. Sufficient lateral exposure is recommended to expand the surgical site such that blood pooling over the DRG is minimized.66.Begin nerve exposure. Make a 15 mm incision on the lateral thigh skin and identify the sciatic nerve between the vastus lateralis and the biceps femoris muscles (Figure 6I).67.Expose and isolate the sciatic nerve approximately 20 mm from the surrounding tissue using the glass rod with a bent tip (Figures 6J and 6K).68.Fix the animal in the stereotaxic frame.Note: Make sure the endotracheal tube and the intravenous catheter do not become dislodged while securing the animal in the stereotaxic frame. After securing the fixation, check the ventilation status again. It is recommended to temporarily remove the electrocardiogram, oxygen saturation, and rectal temperature probes.69.Suspend the limbs with the stereotaxic frame to keep the body and limbs elevated.70.Clamp both the upper thoracic spine and the base of the tail to immobilize the animal.71.Raise the skin and muscle around the exposed DRG and nerve and tie them to form a pool filled with mineral oil to protect the exposed DRG and nerve from drying out.Note: Warm the mineral oil to 36–40°C before use, and periodically check the temperature and volume, as both may decrease over time.72.Switch the anesthesia to intravenous administration of thiopental sodium (10 mg/kg).73.Administer rocuronium bromide intravenously (10 mg/kg) to achieve neuromuscular blockade.74.Open the dura with a sharp needle.75.Identify the L4 and L5 dorsal roots and separate them from the surrounding root using the glass rod with a bent tip (Figure 6F).Note: To prevent drying out, perform this procedure in the mineral oil.

•Prepare the surgical tool used to isolate the nerve and the root: a glass rod with a bent tip. To make this tool. cut the tip of a Pasteur pipette and bend it to a 90-degree angle using a gas burner.

•Prepare the stimulus electrode: a bipolar cuff electrode (Figure 2C). It is recommended to use a silicone tube cuff with an inner diameter of 1.5 mm, equipped with platinum electrodes that are 0.5 mm wide and spaced 1 mm apart (Figure 2E). We commissioned the fabrication to Unique Medical (Tokyo, Japan).

•Prepare the recording electrode: a bipolar hook electrode (Figure 2D). It is recommended to use a stainless-steel rod coated with cashew lacquer for electrical insulation, with the tip bent at 2.5 mm and the coating removed to expose the electrode (Figure 2F). We commissioned the fabrication to Unique Medical (Tokyo, Japan).

•Prepare the surgical tool used to isolate the nerve and the root: a glass rod with a bent tip. To make this tool. cut the tip of a Pasteur pipette and bend it to a 90-degree angle using a gas burner.•Prepare the stimulus electrode: a bipolar cuff electrode (Figure 2C). It is recommended to use a silicone tube cuff with an inner diameter of 1.5 mm, equipped with platinum electrodes that are 0.5 mm wide and spaced 1 mm apart (Figure 2E). We commissioned the fabrication to Unique Medical (Tokyo, Japan).•Prepare the recording electrode: a bipolar hook electrode (Figure 2D). It is recommended to use a stainless-steel rod coated with cashew lacquer for electrical insulation, with the tip bent at 2.5 mm and the coating removed to expose the electrode (Figure 2F). We commissioned the fabrication to Unique Medical (Tokyo, Japan).
Note: Cashew lacquer was used to insulate the electrode except for the tip, to prevent unintended current leakage. This coating provides durable and water-resistant insulation under physiological conditions.
The materials used in this study are available upon reasonable request.
